ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for crispy fried baby corn. FOODON_03301710 (corn food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers baby corn as a raw commodity. No specific code exists for batter-fried crispy baby corn preparation; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for crispy fried baby corn as a prepared dish.

allergens: gluten (if wheat flour used in batter)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of crispy baby corn is approximately 200 g, comprising 8–12 pieces of batter-fried baby corn served with a dipping chutney or sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Baby corn, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11026) and chickpea flour / rice flour batter composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries; deep-frying oil absorption factor applied per standard culinary reference
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for crispy fried baby corn exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for baby corn (raw), chickpea flour batter, and a standard deep-frying oil absorption factor of approximately 8–12% of total weight. Energy and macros are consistent with comparable deep-fried vegetable fritter entries in USDA FoodData Central (e.g., vegetable pakora analogues). Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of corn and corn-based products; chickpea flour (besan) GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crispy fried baby corn. GI is estimated at 52 based on analogous foods in the University of Sydney GI Database: chickpea flour (besan) batter has a GI of approximately 39–45; baby corn (sweet corn analogue) has a GI of approximately 52–55; the composite batter-fried preparation is estimated at GI 52 (low), reflecting the moderating effect of chickpea flour on glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (52 × 27.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 28.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning and batter
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving of crispy baby corn contributes approximately 640 mg sodium, representing about 28% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index and significant carbohydrate load from batter
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 52 and a glycemic load of approximately 28.6 g per 200 g serving, crispy baby corn poses a moderate glycaemic burden for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; limiting portion size to 100 g and pairing with a high-fibre accompaniment can help attenuate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying oil absorption increasing total and unsaturated fat intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crispy baby corn is deep-fried, contributing approximately 10.5 g fat per 100 g; while saturated fat is relatively low at 1.2 g per 100 g, the total fat and sodium content warrant moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, and baked or air-fried preparation is a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Baby Corn is a popular Indian appetiser and snack consisting of tender whole baby corn cobs coated in a spiced batter — typically made from chickpea flour (besan) and rice flour seasoned with cumin, chilli powder, turmeric, and coriander — and deep-fried until golden and crunchy. The dish is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and South Asia as a starter or side dish. The exterior is light and crisp while the interior remains tender and mildly sweet, characteristic of young baby corn. It is commonly accompanied by mint chutney, tamarind sauce, or a yoghurt-based dip. Nutritionally, a 200 g restaurant serving provides approximately 440 kcal, 10.4 g protein, 21 g fat, and 55 g carbohydrate, with a moderate glycaemic load. The chickpea flour batter contributes dietary fibre and plant protein, partially offsetting the energy density from frying. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary preferences. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on seasoning, and individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease should exercise portion control. The estimated glycaemic index of 52 places this dish in the low-GI category, though the glycaemic load per full serving is significant.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy baby corn?
Can diabetics eat crispy baby corn?
What flour is used in crispy baby corn batter?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for crispy fried baby corn exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for raw baby corn, chickpea flour batter composition, and a standard deep-frying oil absorption factor. GI is estimated from analogous foods in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite estimated record.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (baby corn raw, chickpea flour, rice flour) with a deep-frying oil absorption factor of approximately 10% by weight; no direct approved-source entry for this dish exists. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as vegetable frying oils used in Indian restaurant preparation do not contribute significant industrial trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central vegetable oil entries. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as baby corn and chickpea flour contain no meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as all ingredients are plant-based. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not sentinel values. 7. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chickpea flour and sweet corn; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 8. The field dataing (Dugout - Circular Road, field survey data) is preserved as provenance context but is not used as a nutritional source. 9. Price data (SGD 17.50) is preserved from input but not used in nutritional analysis.
